summary:
The Swanson and Hugh Butler Reservoirs Land Conveyances Act (Public Law 118-185) directs the Secretary of the Interior, acting through the Commissioner of Reclamation, to transfer certain federal land at Swanson Reservoir and Hugh Butler Reservoir in Nebraska to Hitchcock County and Frontier County.

The land involved consists of five parcels: permitted cabin lots and concession lands at each reservoir, plus concession land at Swanson, totaling roughly 77 acres. Within three years of enactment, the Secretary must make good-faith efforts to negotiate and enter into "title transfer agreements" with each county to convey all federal interest in the applicable parcels. The agreements must require that each parcel be conveyed in whole, not subdivided, and that the receiving county demonstrate the technical and financial capacity to operate and maintain the land and bring it into compliance with state fire, safety, and health codes within two years of conveyance.

As consideration, each county must pay the fair market value of the land, determined by a third-party appraisal conducted under the Uniform Standards of Professional Appraisal Practice. Permit-holder improvements are excluded from the appraised value, while anticipated future federal revenues (such as water service contracts and aid to irrigation) are included. Payments are made available to the Secretary, subject to appropriation, for operating the two reservoirs. Counties must also pay survey and administrative costs in advance.

Counties must manage the land for substantially its current uses or for recreation, public access, fish and wildlife habitat, or preservation of natural character. Reconveyance is generally prohibited, except at no cost to a Nebraska public or governmental entity meeting specified conditions.

Conveyances are subject to existing rights, reservoir operational requirements, flowage easements, mineral rights, existing permits, and deed restrictions barring new permanent structures below specified elevations. The United States is held harmless from various liabilities, and temporary flooding is not deemed a taking.

Existing management agreements remain in force until conveyance or three years after enactment. If no agreement is reached within three years, the Secretary continues managing the unaffected land under applicable law. Approved December 23, 2024.
